programs.name: North Carolina Homestead Exemption

eligibility.age: 65 years old as of January 1 of the tax year, OR totally and permanently disabled (any age)

income_limits.description: Annual household income cannot exceed the limit set by NC Department of Revenue on or before July 1 each year
income_limits.historical_reference: $38,800 mentioned in some county materials (may reflect prior year)
income_limits.note: Income limits vary annually and are set by state; check with your county assessor for current year

eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits specified in statute
other_requirements: Must be a North Carolina resident
Must own and occupy the property as a permanent residence on or before January 1 of the tax year
Property must be primary residence only (not vacation homes, rental properties, or other real estate investments)
Property can be a single-family residence, manufactured home, condominium, or townhome
Property includes dwelling, dwelling site (not to exceed 1 acre), and related improvements (garage, carport, storage building)

benefits.value: Property tax exclusion of the greater of $25,000 OR 50% of the appraised value of the residence[1][3][4]
benefits.additional_note: This is a property tax exclusion, not a bankruptcy exemption. For bankruptcy protection, the homestead exemption is $35,000 for those under 65, and up to $60,000 for those 65 and older under certain conditions[2][7]

methods: In-person at county tax assessor's office (example: Iredell County Tax Department, 135 E Water St, Room 100, Statesville, NC 28677)[5]
By mail to your county tax assessor
Online through your county's website (varies by county)
application.processing_time: Not specified in search results; contact your county assessor for specific timeline
forms: County-specific homestead exclusion application form (name/number varies by county; contact your assessor)
documents: Proof of age (if 65+) or disability certification (if disabled)
For disabled veterans: VA disability certification or evidence of benefits received for specially adapted housing under 38 USC 2101

geography.restrictions: Available statewide in North Carolina; administered by individual county tax assessors
geography.regional_variations: Application procedures and deadlines may vary slightly by county; income limits are set statewide but applied at county level
geography.offices_or_providers: Each county has its own tax assessor's office; contact your specific county for application location and procedures

programs.official_source: North Carolina General Statute § 105-277.1; county tax assessor offices
gotchas: Income limits are set annually by NC Department of Revenue (as of July 1 each year) and change year to year—verify current limits with your county[6]
This is a property tax exclusion program, NOT the same as the homestead exemption used in bankruptcy proceedings[2][7]
You cannot receive other property tax relief if you receive this exclusion[3]
Property must be occupied as primary residence; temporary absences for health reasons or nursing home confinement are permitted if spouse or dependent occupies it[3]
The exclusion is permanent once approved, but you must meet eligibility requirements each year[1]
If you recently moved assets into your home to avoid creditors, courts may disallow the exemption[2]

For those 65+, there is also a separate Tax Limitation program (different from the Elderly/Disabled Exclusion) that requires annual application by June 1 deadline and has different income tiers (4% tax on income $0-$38,800; 5% on $38,801-$55,050; does not qualify over $55,050)[1]
Disabled veterans have a separate, more generous program: $45,000 exclusion with NO age or income requirements[1][4][6]
programs.data_shape_notes: This program has multiple tiers: (1) Elderly/Disabled Exclusion (one-time application, permanent benefit, income-based), (2) Elderly/Disabled Tax Limitation (annual application required, income-tiered tax rates), and (3) Disabled Veteran Exclusion (no income/age limits). The search results primarily address the Elderly/Disabled Exclusion. Income limits are set statewide but administered by county. Benefits are fixed dollar amounts or percentages, not scaled by household size. Application is decentralized to county level.

## Key Guidance for Families

**Start here:** Contact your county tax assessor's office directly. They administer this program and can provide current income limits, application forms, and specific deadlines for your county.

**Critical distinction:** The "homestead exemption" has two meanings in North Carolina—this property tax exclusion program is different from the homestead exemption used to protect home equity in bankruptcy[2][7].

**For disabled veterans:** If your elderly loved one is an honorably discharged veteran with a service-connected disability, they may qualify for a more generous program ($45,000 exclusion with no income or age limits)[1][4].

**Income verification:** Bring documentation of last year's household income. Income limits change annually, so verify the current year's limit before applying[6].

programs.name: North Carolina Seniors Farmers' Market Nutrition Program

eligibility.income_limits: Household income at or below 185% of the Federal Poverty Level. Examples: $2,322 or less per month for 1-person household; $3,152 or less per month for 2-person household (2024 figures); $2,413/$3,261 cited for 2025 in some regions[1][2][3]. Full table varies by year and household size—check current FPL guidelines via local agency.
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits mentioned in program details[1][2][3][4][5].
other_requirements: Reside in a participating county (not statewide; available in 49-57 of 100 counties)[1][2]
Not an entitlement program—vouchers not guaranteed even if eligible due to limited funding[1]

benefits.value: $50 in vouchers per eligible senior to purchase fresh fruits, vegetables, raw honey, and fresh-cut herbs at certified farmers' markets, roadside stands, or CSAs. Minimum $20, maximum $50 per federal guidelines, redeemable July 1–September 30[1][3][5].

methods: Fill out NC SFMNP Voucher Interest Form (pre-application to join interest list; notified in June of local agency contacts)[2]

Contact local agency in participating county for eligibility screening, application, and voucher distribution (varies by county—no central statewide phone/website listed; e.g., NC DHHS Division of Aging leads, partners with county DSS)[3]
In-person at participating farmers' markets or local agencies (e.g., Mecklenburg County DSS)[3]
application.processing_time: Interest list notifications in June; applications open around then for July start—no specific processing timeline stated[2].
application.waitlist: Not guaranteed due to limited funds; interest form acts as pre-waitlist mechanism[1][2].
forms: County-specific application via local agency (no statewide form number specified)
documents: Proof of residency in participating county
Proof of household income ≤185% FPL (e.g., pay stubs, tax returns); some agencies may accept proof of enrollment in SNAP/CSFP[5]

geography.restrictions: Limited to participating counties only (e.g., 49 in 2024, including Chatham, Mecklenburg/Triangle areas; check annual map/list via local agency or NC DHHS)[1][2].
geography.regional_variations: Administered by local agencies (e.g., county DSS, food councils); different participating markets (e.g., Charlotte Regional Farmers Market, Pittsboro); varying availability and partners by county[1][3].
offices_or_providers: Local county agencies (e.g., Mecklenburg County DSS), NC DHHS Division of Aging, certified farmers' markets like Pittsboro, Charlotte Regional[1][3]

programs.official_source: https://www.ncdhhs.gov (NC DHHS Division of Aging leads; specific SFMNP page referenced in sources, also check county CES or agr.gov for updates)[1][3]
gotchas: Not available in all 100 NC counties—must confirm participating county first[1][2]
Limited funding means no guarantee even if eligible[1]
Summer-only (July 1–Sept 30); vouchers unusable outside season or at non-certified vendors[1][3][5]
Distinct from WIC FMNP (for pregnant/postpartum/children) and SNAP doubling[2][7]
Income limits based on annual FPL updates—2024/2025 figures differ slightly by source[1][2][3]
programs.data_shape_notes: County-restricted (not statewide); fixed $50 voucher regardless of household size; no asset test; pre-application interest form required due to funding limits; varies by local agency administration

programs.name: Project C.A.R.E.

eligibility.income_limits: Targets low-income families; most recipients are just above Medicaid eligibility or on waiting lists for Medicaid/other public funds. No exact dollar amounts or household size table specified in sources.
other_requirements: Person with dementia must have confirmed Alzheimer's disease or other types of dementia (memory loss and confusion) by caregiver, physician, or healthcare provider[2][3]

Residence of the person with dementia must be in one of the counties served by the regional host agency (e.g., 29 counties in Northeastern NC Project C.A.R.E.: Northampton, Hertford, Gates, Currituck, Camden, Pasquotank, Chowan, Washington, Tyrrell, Dare, Hyde, Halifax, Bertie, Nash, Edgecombe, Martin, Wilson, Pitt, Beaufort, Wayne, Greene, Lenoir, Craven, Pamlico, Jones, Duplin, Onslow, Carteret[2]; program serves 100 counties statewide[2])
Caregiver must be at least 18 years old[3]
Caregivers in Medicaid CAP/DA are eligible for all benefits except additional respite funding; may receive respite while on CAP/DA waitlist[2]

benefits.value: Care consultation, individualized care planning, caregiver assessment, dementia-specific education and training, information and referrals, connections to community resources (e.g., Area Agencies on Aging, Family Caregiver Support Program), limited self-directed respite care funds (vouchers for in-home respite or group settings like Adult Day Care/Health; excludes additional respite for CAP/DA enrollees)[2][3][5][6]

methods: Phone: Regional intake lines (e.g., Just1Call at 704-432-1111 for Mecklenburg[6]; State Director Laura Jane Strunin at 984-365-6992[3])

Contact regional host agencies: Northeastern (Albemarle/Mid-East Commission[2]), Southeastern (Cape Fear Council of Governments[5]), South Central (Mecklenburg County DSS[6]), Central (Duke Dementia Family Support Program[3][8]), Foothills (Western Piedmont Council of Government[2]), Western (Land of Sky Regional Council[2])
No statewide online application or specific form name listed; applications go through regional Family Consultants or host agencies
application.waitlist: Funds are limited; prioritizes caregivers in crisis; may involve waitlists similar to Medicaid CAP/DA[2][5]
documents: Confirmation of dementia diagnosis (from caregiver, physician, or healthcare provider)[2]

geography.restrictions: Serves 100 counties through 6 regional host agencies; county coverage assigned to specific regions (e.g., 29 counties in Northeast[2])
geography.regional_variations: Delivered by regional host agencies (Northeastern: Albemarle Commission; Southeastern: Cape Fear; South Central: Mecklenburg DSS; Central: Duke; Foothills: Western Piedmont; Western: Land of Sky); different counties served per region, potentially varying wait times and availability due to limited funds[2][3]
offices_or_providers: Northeastern: Albemarle Commission Area Agency on Aging (Hertford)[2][3]
Southeastern: Cape Fear Council of Governments (Wilmington)[3][5]
South Central: Mecklenburg County Department of Social Services (Charlotte)[2][3][6]
Central: Duke Dementia Family Support Program (Durham)[2][3][8]
Foothills: Western Piedmont Council of Government (Hickory)[2][3]
Western: Land of Sky Area Agency on Aging (Asheville)[2][3]

gotchas: Limited funds; respite vouchers subject to availability and not additional for CAP/DA enrollees[2][5]
Must reside in specific regional counties; not all NC counties may be covered uniformly[2]
Targets low-income/rural/minority caregivers just above Medicaid; those who can pay may get consultation only[4]
Apply through regional host agency, not centralized statewide process[1][2][3]
programs.data_shape_notes: Regionally administered through 6 host agencies covering 100 counties; respite funds limited and prioritized by crisis need; no fixed income/asset tables or age minimum for care recipient

programs.name: NC Lifespan Respite Program

eligibility.income_limits: No specific dollar amounts or household size table stated; priority given to those with greatest economic need[1][3][5]
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits or exemptions mentioned[1][3][5]
other_requirements: Providing unpaid care to older adult (60+) or adult/minor of any age with disabilities requiring regular assistance with activities of daily living[1][3]
Neither caregiver nor care recipient receiving ongoing publicly-funded in-home care or respite care[1][3]
Must use voucher funds within 90 days[3]
Priority: greatest economic need, provided direct care for at least 3 months in home-like setting, no publicly-funded respite in last 3 months, emergency/extenuating circumstances[3]

benefits.value: $500 reimbursement voucher (some sources state up to $750 per calendar year); flexible for hiring agency, facility, or individual for respite care[1][5]

methods: Online: www.highcountryaging.org/services/lifespan-respite-project (secure application; must be submitted by referring agency/professional, not directly by caregiver)[1][5][6]
Phone: 828-265-5434 ext. 139 (Pat Guarnieri or Tammy Nelson); email: pguarnieri@regiond.org or lifespan@hccog.org[1][4][5]
application.processing_time: Takes only minutes to complete online application; further contact handled by High Country Area Agency on Aging (no specific processing timeline stated)[1][5]
forms: Secure online referral application (completed by health/human service professionals on behalf of caregiver); eligibility pre-check available[5]
documents: Not explicitly listed; instructions and additional criteria on website[1][5]

geography.regional_variations: Administered by High Country Area Agency on Aging; may involve local referring agencies like counselors, senior centers, area agencies on aging (potential regional differences in providers or access via referrals)[1][5]
offices_or_providers: High Country Area Agency on Aging (primary administrator)[1][5]

gotchas: Applications must come from a referring agency/professional (caregivers cannot apply directly)[1][5][7]
Not available if receiving ongoing publicly-funded in-home or respite care[1][3]
Funds must be used within 90 days[3]
Reimbursement-based voucher (pay provider first, then reimbursed)[1][4]
programs.data_shape_notes: Referral-only applications via professionals; priority-based rather than strict income/asset tests; administered regionally by High Country AAA with statewide NC resident eligibility
